name: Brain Teasers
description: Logic puzzles, lateral thinking, and trick questions with a lekker SA twist!
author: Game Night Crew

questions.question: If you have 3 boerewors rolls and I take away 2, how many do YOU have?
questions.explanation: You took 2, so YOU have 2! The classic trick — the question asks how many YOU have, not how many are left.

questions.question: A farmer has 17 sheep. All but 9 run away. How many sheep does the farmer have left?
questions.explanation: "All but 9" means 9 remain. Don't overthink it, china!

questions.question: What comes once in a minute, twice in a moment, but never in a thousand years?
questions.explanation: The letter 'M' appears once in 'minute', twice in 'moment', and zero times in 'a thousand years'. It's about spelling, not time!

questions.question: Is it legal in South Africa for a man to marry his widow's sister?
questions.explanation: If he has a widow, he's dead! Dead men can't marry anyone, bru.

questions.question: How many months in a year have 28 days?
questions.explanation: ALL 12 months have at least 28 days! February has exactly 28 (or 29), but every month has 28 days in it.

questions.question: A Joburg taxi driver is going the wrong way down a one-way street. He passes a traffic cop but isn't stopped or fined. Why?
questions.explanation: He was walking down the street! Nobody said he was driving. Classic misdirection.

questions.question: I have cities but no houses, forests but no trees, and water but no fish. What am I?
questions.explanation: A map has cities, forests, and water — but none of the actual things! Pretty clever, ne?

questions.question: If there are 6 apples in a basket and you take away 4, how many apples do you have?
questions.explanation: You took 4, so you have 4 apples. The basket still has 2, but the question asks about YOU.

questions.question: What gets wetter the more it dries?
questions.explanation: A towel! The more drying it does, the wetter it gets. Every South African oke knows this from beach day.

questions.question: Two fathers and two sons go fishing at Langebaan. They each catch one fish. They come home with exactly 3 fish. How is this possible?
options: There are only 3 people — grandpa, dad, and son
One person threw a fish back
questions.explanation: It's a grandfather, his son, and his grandson. The middle man is both a father AND a son. Three people, three fish!

questions.question: You're running a Comrades Marathon. You overtake the person in 2nd place. What position are you now in?
questions.explanation: If you overtake the person in 2nd, you take their position — you're now in 2nd! You didn't overtake 1st place.

questions.question: A man builds a house with all 4 sides facing south. A bear walks by. What colour is the bear?
questions.explanation: The only place you can build a house where all 4 sides face south is the North Pole. The bear is a polar bear — so it's white!

questions.question: A man pushes his car to a hotel and loses all his money. What happened?
options: His car was stolen at the hotel
The hotel charged him for parking
He bet on a car race and lost
questions.explanation: He's playing Monopoly! He landed on a hotel property and went bankrupt. The 'car' is his game piece. Aha!

questions.question: A plane crashes exactly on the border of Limpopo and Mpumalanga. Where do they bury the survivors?
questions.explanation: You don't bury SURVIVORS! They're alive, bru. Listen carefully to the question.

questions.question: Before Mafadi Peak was discovered, what was the highest mountain in South Africa?
questions.explanation: Mafadi Peak! It was always the highest — 'discovering' it didn't change that. A mountain doesn't grow taller when someone finds it, ne?

questions.question: A woman has two children. One of them is a boy. What are the chances the other child is also a boy?
questions.explanation: Not 50%! The possible combinations with at least one boy are: Boy-Boy, Boy-Girl, Girl-Boy. Only 1 out of 3 has two boys. So it's 33%. The 'at least one boy' condition changes everything!

questions.question: A doctor gives you 3 pills and says to take one every 30 minutes. How long do the pills last?
questions.explanation: You take the first pill immediately (0 min), the second at 30 min, and the third at 60 min. That's 1 hour total, not 1.5! Most people multiply 3 x 30 without thinking.

questions.question: What disappears as soon as you say its name?
questions.explanation: Silence! The moment you say 'silence', you've broken it. A proper brain-bender, that one.

questions.question: I speak without a mouth and hear without ears. I have no body, but I come alive with the wind. What am I?
questions.explanation: An echo! It 'speaks' back to you and 'hears' your voice, but has no physical form. Wind can carry echoes through valleys.

questions.question: You walk into a dark room during loadshedding with a match. There's a candle, a paraffin lamp, and a braai. What do you light first?
questions.explanation: You light the match first! You need it to light anything else. Classic trick question for load shedding season.

questions.question: A man is found dead in a field with an unopened package next to him. No one else is around. How did he die?
options: He was poisoned by the package contents
He tripped and hit his head
questions.explanation: The unopened package was his parachute! He was skydiving and it failed to open. The 'package' is the key clue — lateral thinking at its finest.

questions.question: The more you take, the more you leave behind. What am I?
questions.explanation: Footsteps! Every step you take leaves another one behind you. Simple but brilliant once you see it.

questions.question: A man lives on the 10th floor. Every day he takes the lift down to the ground floor to go to work. When he returns, he takes the lift to the 7th floor and walks up 3 flights. Why?
options: The lift is broken above floor 7
He's too short to reach the button for floor 10
questions.explanation: He's too short to reach the 10th floor button! He can press the ground floor button (bottom) and reach up to floor 7. On rainy days, he uses his umbrella to press 10. Classic lateral thinking puzzle!

questions.question: You're in a race and overtake the last person. What position are you in? (Enter 0 if it's impossible)
questions.explanation: It's impossible! You can't overtake the person in last place — if you're behind them, then THEY'RE not last, you are. The scenario is impossible.

questions.question: A shepherd has 20 sheep. A disease kills all but 7. How many are left alive?
questions.explanation: "All but 7" means 7 survive. Same trick as before — read carefully! 7 sheep are left.

questions.question: You have two ropes that each burn in exactly 1 hour (but unevenly). To measure exactly 45 minutes, put these steps in order:
options: Light rope 1 from BOTH ends AND rope 2 from ONE end
Light the OTHER end of rope 2
questions.explanation: Step 1: Light rope 1 from both ends + rope 2 from one end simultaneously. Step 2: Rope 1 burns out in 30 minutes (burning from both sides). Step 3: Immediately light rope 2's other end. Step 4: Rope 2 has 30 min of burn left, but from both ends = 15 min more. Total: 45 minutes!

questions.question: Three switches outside a room each control one of three lightbulbs inside. You can flip switches as much as you want, but you can only enter the room once. How do you figure out which switch controls which bulb?
options: Turn all on, then off one at a time
Turn on switch 1, wait, turn it off, turn on switch 2, then enter and feel the bulbs
Use a mirror under the door
questions.explanation: Turn on switch 1 for 10 minutes, then turn it off. Turn on switch 2. Enter the room: the lit bulb is switch 2, the warm bulb is switch 1, and the cold dark bulb is switch 3. Heat is the key!

questions.question: A windowless room has 3 lightbulbs inside and 3 switches outside. You need to match each switch to its bulb. You can only enter the room ONCE. A friend suggests feeling the bulbs for warmth. But what if all 3 bulbs are LED (no heat)? What strategy works?
options: Turn on switch 1 and 2. Enter, note which are on. Come out — wait, you can't re-enter!
Turn on switch 1. Enter and unscrew bulb 2. Come out, turn off 1, turn on 2. Enter — wait, only one entry!
Turn on switch 1 halfway, switch 2 fully. Dim bulb = switch 1, bright = switch 2, off = switch 3
questions.explanation: Use a dimmer! Turn switch 1 halfway (dim), switch 2 fully on, switch 3 off. Enter: the dim bulb is switch 1, the bright bulb is switch 2, the dark one is switch 3. You gotta think outside the box when the heat trick won't work!

questions.question: You're in a dark room during loadshedding. You have one match. The room has a newspaper, a candle, a gas stove, and a fireplace full of wood. Which do you light first?
options: The newspaper, to see the room
The gas stove, for warmth and light
questions.explanation: The match! You always have to light the match before you can light anything else. Sometimes the simplest answer is right in front of you, ne?

questions.question: You're on a game show with 3 doors. Behind one is a Krugerrand, behind the others, nothing. You pick door 1. The host opens door 3 (empty). Should you switch to door 2?
questions.explanation: The Monty Hall problem! Your original choice had 1/3 chance. The other doors had 2/3 combined. When the host reveals an empty door, that 2/3 collapses onto the remaining door. ALWAYS switch!

questions.question: A man is looking at a painting in a gallery. Someone asks who's in the portrait. He says: 'Brothers and sisters I have none, but that man's father is my father's son.' Who is in the painting?
questions.explanation: 'My father's son' = himself (he has no brothers). So 'that man's father is ME.' The man in the painting is his son! This one trips up almost everyone.

questions.question: Two people are born at the same time, to the same parents, but they're not twins. How?
options: One is from a test tube
questions.explanation: They're triplets (or quadruplets, etc.)! Same parents, same time — just not twins because there are more than two of them.

questions.question: I can be cracked, made, told, and played. What am I?
questions.explanation: A joke! You crack a joke, make a joke, tell a joke, and play a joke. None of the other options fit all four verbs.

questions.question: A woman shoots her husband, then holds him under water for 5 minutes. Next, she hangs him. But 10 minutes later, they go out for dinner together. How?
options: She shot, developed, and hung a photograph of him
He was wearing a bulletproof vest
questions.explanation: She's a photographer! She SHOT his photo, DEVELOPED it in water, and HUNG it up to dry. Classic misdirection.

questions.question: Put these steps in the correct order to solve the classic river crossing puzzle: A farmer needs to get a jackal, a chicken, and mielies across a river (boat fits farmer + 1 item). 1) Take mielies across 2) Take chicken across 3) Bring chicken back 4) Take jackal across
questions.explanation: First take the chicken (safe to leave jackal with mielies). Then take the jackal, but bring the chicken back (can't leave jackal with chicken). Take the mielies across. Finally, go back for the chicken. Sorted!

questions.question: You're driving a bus from Cape Town. At the first stop, 10 people get on. At the second stop, 5 get off and 3 get on. At the third stop, 7 get on. What is the name of the bus driver?
options: The driver's name is your name
questions.explanation: Read it again: 'YOU'RE driving a bus.' The driver is YOU! The numbers are just there to distract you from the very first sentence. Got you, ne?

questions.question: Put these logic puzzle steps in order to determine who owns the fish: 1) The Brit lives in the red house. 2) The Swede keeps dogs. 3) The Dane drinks tea. 4) The green house is left of the white. Which nationality is stated FIRST in the classic Einstein puzzle?
options: The Norwegian lives in the first house
The Brit lives in the red house
questions.explanation: In Einstein's riddle, the clues are ordered: Norwegian first (house 1), then Brit (red house), then Swede (dogs), then German (Prince cigarettes). The German owns the fish! This is one of the hardest logic puzzles ever made.

questions.question: Five people were eating apples. A finished before B. C finished before D. D finished before B. E finished after C but before A. Who finished eating last?
questions.explanation: Let's trace it out: C finished first, then E, then D, then A, then B finished last. The order is C > E > D > A > B. You have to chain the clues together carefully!

questions.question: A man is looking at a photograph. Someone asks, 'Whose picture are you looking at?' He replies: 'Brothers and sisters I have none, but this man's father is my father's son.' Who is in the photo?
questions.explanation: 'My father's son' = himself (no brothers). So 'this man's father is me.' The man in the photo is his son!

questions.question: Three boxes are labelled 'Apples', 'Oranges', and 'Apples & Oranges'. ALL labels are wrong. You pick one fruit from one box. Which box should you pick from to figure out all three?
questions.explanation: Pick from 'Apples & Oranges'. Since ALL labels are wrong, this box must contain ONLY apples or ONLY oranges. If you pull out an apple, it's the Apples box. Then you can deduce the rest. Logic chain, bru!

questions.question: You're at a braai and someone says: 'I always lie.' Is that statement true or false?
options: It's a paradox — it can't be either
questions.explanation: Classic liar's paradox! If the statement is true, then he always lies — so the statement must be false. But if it's false, he doesn't always lie — so it could be true. It's a logical paradox with no resolution. Brain = melted!

questions.question: A king sentences a prisoner: 'You will be executed tomorrow, but you will NOT know the day of your execution until the executioner knocks on your door.' The prisoner reasons he can't be executed on Friday (the last day, he'd know by Thursday night). Then Thursday is eliminated, then Wednesday... He concludes he's safe. What happens?
options: He's right — the sentence is impossible
He's executed on Wednesday and is genuinely surprised
The king pardons him for his clever logic
He's executed on a random day and IS surprised, disproving his logic
questions.explanation: The Unexpected Hanging Paradox! The prisoner's reasoning seems sound step by step, but by convincing himself it can't happen, he creates the very surprise the king promised. When the executioner knocks on, say, Wednesday — he IS surprised! His logic defeated itself.

questions.question: You meet two guards. One ALWAYS tells the truth, one ALWAYS lies. You don't know which is which. One guard says: 'We are both liars.' Which guard said it?
questions.explanation: The truth-teller can't say 'we are both liars' because that would be a lie. The liar CAN say it, because 'we are both liars' is false (they're not both liars), and the liar always says false things. So the LIAR said it!

questions.question: There are 3 boxes: one has 2 gold coins, one has 2 silver coins, one has 1 of each. Labels are ALL wrong. You can draw 1 coin from 1 box. From which labelled box should you draw to figure out all three?
questions.explanation: Draw from the 'Gold/Silver' box. Since ALL labels are wrong, this box is either Gold/Gold or Silver/Silver. If you draw gold, it must be Gold/Gold. Then the box labelled 'Gold/Gold' must be Silver/Silver (can't be Gold/Gold), and the last is Gold/Silver.

questions.question: A room has 100 people. 99% of them are wearing hats. How many hat-wearers must leave so that only 98% of people in the room are wearing hats?
questions.explanation: Start: 99 hat-wearers, 1 without. Need hat-wearers to be 98% of the room. If 50 leave, you have 49 hat-wearers and 1 without = 50 people. 49/50 = 98%. You need to remove FIFTY people to drop just one percent. Mind-blowing!

questions.question: You're in a pitch-dark room. On a table are 52 cards, and you know exactly 13 are face-up. You can't see or feel which are face-up. How do you split them into two groups guaranteed to have the same number of face-up cards?
options: Deal them alternating into two piles
Take any 13 cards and flip them all over
Shuffle them and split in half
It's impossible without seeing the cards
questions.explanation: Take ANY 13 cards from the deck and flip them all. If your group has X face-up cards, the other group has 13-X. After flipping your 13, your group also has 13-X face-up cards. Both groups match! Pure logic, no luck needed.

questions.question: You have 12 identical-looking coins. One is counterfeit and weighs differently (you don't know if it's heavier or lighter). Using a balance scale exactly 3 times, can you always find the counterfeit coin AND tell if it's heavier or lighter?
options: Yes, by dividing into groups of 4-4-4
No, 3 weighings aren't enough for 12 coins
Yes, but only if you get lucky
Yes, but only if you know it's heavier
questions.explanation: Yes! Divide into 3 groups of 4. Weigh group A vs group B. Each weighing has 3 outcomes (left heavy, balanced, right heavy), giving 3x3x3 = 27 possible result paths — enough to identify all 24 possibilities (12 coins x heavier or lighter). The strategy is brilliant!

questions.question: You're in a room with 2 doors. One leads to freedom, one to death. Two guards: one always lies, one always tells truth. You don't know which is which. You can ask ONE question to ONE guard. What do you ask?
options: Ask either guard: 'Which door would the OTHER guard say leads to freedom?' Then choose the opposite door.
Ask the truth-teller which door leads to freedom
Ask either guard: 'Are you the liar?'
questions.explanation: Ask either guard: 'Which door would the other guard say leads to freedom?' The answer will always point to the death door (double negation: truth-teller reports liar's lie, liar lies about truth-teller's truth). Choose the OTHER door!

questions.question: There are 10 people in a room. Everyone claims to be a truth-teller, but some are liars. Person A says 'There is at least 1 liar here.' Person B says 'There are at least 2 liars.' Person C says 'At least 3 liars.' ... Person J says 'All 10 are liars.' How many liars are there?
questions.explanation: If there are 5 liars, then persons A-E (at least 1 through 5 liars) are telling the truth, and persons F-J (at least 6 through 10 liars) are lying. That's exactly 5 truth-tellers and 5 liars — self-consistent! Any other number creates a contradiction.

questions.question: In the blue-eyed islanders puzzle: 100 people on an island, all with blue eyes but none know their own eye colour. They can see everyone else. A visitor says 'at least one of you has blue eyes.' Assuming perfect logic, what happens?
options: Nothing — they already knew others had blue eyes
Only 1 person leaves on the first night
questions.explanation: All 100 leave on night 100! The visitor's statement creates common knowledge. By induction: if 1 person had blue eyes, they'd leave night 1. If 2, they'd leave night 2 (each sees 1 and waits). Extending to 100, they all leave night 100. Mind-bending!

questions.question: A census taker visits a woman. She says she has 3 children. 'What are their ages?' he asks. She replies: 'The product of their ages is 36. The sum equals the house number next door.' He looks at the house number and says 'I still don't know.' She adds: 'The eldest plays piano.' Now he knows. What are the ages?
questions.explanation: The factors of 36 with the same sum are: 1+6+6=13 and 2+2+9=13. Since the census taker STILL didn't know after seeing the sum, the sum must be 13 (the only ambiguous one). The clue 'the ELDEST' means there IS a single oldest child — ruling out 1,6,6 (tied eldest). Answer: 2, 2, 9!

questions.question: You have 1000 bottles of wine. One is poisoned. You have 10 prisoners to test with. A prisoner who drinks poison dies within 24 hours. You have exactly 24 hours to find the poisoned bottle. What's the maximum number of bottles you can test?
questions.explanation: Assign each bottle a unique binary number (10 digits = 10 prisoners). Each prisoner drinks from all bottles where their digit is 1. After 24 hours, the pattern of dead/alive prisoners spells out the poisoned bottle's binary number. 2^10 = 1024 bottles testable with just 10 prisoners! Information theory is beautiful.

questions.question: Two perfectly logical friends, Sipho and Thabo, each have a number on their forehead (positive integers, one is one more than the other). They can see each other's number but not their own. They take turns being asked 'Do you know your number?' Sipho sees 5 on Thabo. Both say 'No' repeatedly until someone says 'Yes'. Who says 'Yes' first and what's their number?
questions.explanation: Sipho sees 5, so he's either 4 or 6. If he were 4, Thabo would see 4 and know he's 3 or 5. But if Thabo were 3, Sipho would've seen 3 and known he's 2 or 4... The chain of 'No' answers eliminates possibilities until Sipho can deduce he must be 6. Pure logical deduction!

questions.question: 100 prisoners each must enter a room with 100 boxes (numbered 1-100), each containing a unique prisoner's number. Each prisoner opens at most 50 boxes to find their own number. If ALL succeed, they go free. They can strategize beforehand but can't communicate once started. What's the best strategy's success rate?
questions.explanation: The mind-blowing answer: each prisoner starts at the box matching their number, then follows the chain (open box N, if it contains M, open box M next). This gives about 31% success! The key insight: all prisoners succeed unless there's a cycle longer than 50. The probability of no long cycle is ~31%. Random guessing gives essentially 0%!

questions.question: In a town, the barber shaves everyone who does not shave themselves, and ONLY those people. Who shaves the barber?
options: Another barber from a different town
It's a paradox — no consistent answer exists
questions.explanation: Russell's Barber Paradox! If the barber shaves himself, he shouldn't (he only shaves those who DON'T shave themselves). If he doesn't shave himself, he should (he shaves ALL who don't shave themselves). It's a logical impossibility — a paradox that shook the foundations of mathematics!
